mathematical-logic.question: Which of the following sentences is a statement in logic?
options: How far is Pune from Mumbai?
The number 7 is a prime number.
mathematical-logic.explanation: A statement must be a declarative sentence that is definitely true or false. Commands, questions, and open sentences with variables (like x + 3 = 10) have no fixed truth value.

mathematical-logic.question: The conjunction p ∧ q is true when:
options: at least one of p, q is true
both p and q are true
p is true and q is false
both p and q are false
mathematical-logic.explanation: By the truth table of conjunction, p ∧ q is true only in the single case where both components are true.

mathematical-logic.question: The negation of the statement "All students are sincere" is:
mathematical-logic.explanation: Negating a universal statement ("for all") gives an existential one ("there exists"): it is enough that at least one student is not sincere.

mathematical-logic.question: The conditional statement p → q is false only when:
options: p is true and q is true
p is false and q is true
p is true and q is false
p is false and q is false
mathematical-logic.explanation: An implication fails only when the hypothesis holds but the conclusion does not — i.e. p true, q false. In every other case it is true.

mathematical-logic.question: The contrapositive of p → q is:
mathematical-logic.explanation: The contrapositive negates both parts and reverses the arrow: ~q → ~p. It is logically equivalent to the original implication.

mathematical-logic.question: The biconditional p ↔ q is true when:
options: p and q have the same truth value
p and q have opposite truth values
p is true, regardless of q
q is true, regardless of p
mathematical-logic.explanation: p ↔ q means "p if and only if q", which holds exactly when both are true or both are false.

mathematical-logic.question: The statement pattern p ∨ ~p is:
mathematical-logic.explanation: Whatever the truth value of p, either p or ~p is true, so p ∨ ~p is true in every row of its truth table — a tautology.

mathematical-logic.question: The negation of p → q is logically equivalent to:
mathematical-logic.explanation: Since p → q ≡ ~p ∨ q, its negation is ~(~p ∨ q) ≡ p ∧ ~q by De Morgan's law.

mathematical-logic.question: The symbol ∃ used in quantified statements is read as:
mathematical-logic.explanation: ∃ is the existential quantifier ("there exists at least one"); ∀ is the universal quantifier ("for all").

mathematical-logic.question: The dual of the statement pattern (p ∧ q) ∨ t (where t is a tautology and c a contradiction) is:
mathematical-logic.explanation: To form the dual, interchange ∧ with ∨ and t with c, leaving the components unchanged: (p ∧ q) ∨ t becomes (p ∨ q) ∧ c.

matrices.question: If A is a matrix of order 2 × 3 and B is of order 3 × 2, then the order of AB is:
matrices.explanation: The product of an m × n matrix with an n × p matrix has order m × p, so AB is 2 × 2.

matrices.question: A square matrix A is called singular if:
matrices.explanation: A matrix is singular when its determinant is zero; such a matrix has no inverse.

matrices.question: If A is a square matrix and I the identity matrix of the same order, then AI equals:
matrices.explanation: The identity matrix acts like the number 1 in multiplication: AI = IA = A.

matrices.question: If A = [[2, 0], [0, 5]], then A⁻¹ is:
matrices.explanation: The inverse of a diagonal matrix (with non-zero diagonal entries) is the diagonal matrix of reciprocals: diag(1/2, 1/5).

matrices.question: For invertible matrices A and B of the same order, (AB)⁻¹ equals:
matrices.explanation: The inverse of a product reverses the order of the factors: (AB)(B⁻¹A⁻¹) = A(BB⁻¹)A⁻¹ = I.

matrices.question: A square matrix A is symmetric if:
matrices.explanation: Symmetric means the matrix equals its own transpose, so the entry in row i, column j equals the entry in row j, column i.

matrices.question: For matrices A and B of the same order, (A + B)ᵀ equals:
matrices.explanation: Transposition distributes over addition: (A + B)ᵀ = Aᵀ + Bᵀ. (It is products that reverse order, not sums.)

matrices.question: For a square matrix A of order n, A · adj(A) equals:
matrices.explanation: The defining property of the adjoint is A·adj(A) = adj(A)·A = |A| I, which is why A⁻¹ = adj(A)/|A| when |A| ≠ 0.

matrices.question: Applying the row operation R₂ → R₂ - 2R₁ to [[1, 2], [2, 7]] gives:
matrices.explanation: New second row = (2, 7) - 2 × (1, 2) = (0, 3); the first row is unchanged.

matrices.question: The system AX = B (A square) has a unique solution X = A⁻¹B provided:
matrices.explanation: The matrix-inversion method needs A⁻¹ to exist, which requires A to be non-singular, i.e. |A| ≠ 0.
